speaker: interviewer
text: Today is August 25th, 1996. And you were telling me about working with the British, after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had asked you the kind of work that you did for UNRRA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was Secretary to the Vice Director, Mr. [? Kidman. ?] And to do the director, Mr. [? Blumberg, ?] who was the Governor of Gibraltar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were Ms-- Ms. [? Steele, ?] also a British woman who was there. And there was-- also because of UNRRA, it was-- we had from France. And we had from-- what-- what else? [INAUDIBLE]? It was the United Nations organization.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, it's United Nations. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I worked with them till 1940, oh, a little longer, I think. Because I got married. So I left my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- I met him in Bergen-Belsen. He came to Bergen-Belsen. But it's a painful thing, I don't want to talk about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned earlier, about being involved with a case against Schnabel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- I-- in-- about 19-- --60, I think, 1955. The German constant-- they knew, probably, about me. I don't know how. But I got a letter from the German consulate. That time I lived in New Rochelle, New York. It was 1964. 1964, or earlier, before that. That they would like me very much to go to Germany. They have Schnabel. They have very few people who survived these camps. And they-- and they know. I don't know how they know. But they know that I was in all those camps. They probably have the list. Well, I guess I asked them. And they would like me to come to Germany, to testify. [SIGHS] I wasn't very happy about it. I had a small child. Mine daughter-- daughter was nine years old, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first I told them, I got in touch with them. I said, I had a-- I was going with mine daughter on a trip. Let my remember. I have these pictures. I have to remember where it was. Venezuela? No, Venezuela was later. I was-- oh. I remember now. See? Because since I had my stroke, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Israel. I had already-- when they call me, the German consulate, I had, already, mine papers and mine [INAUDIBLE]. It was, in January, '65, to go on with the new boat to Israel is [INAUDIBLE] It was the first boat. And I went on that first boat to Israel, with mine daughter. But before I went, and they told me they would like me to testify. So I told them-- they said they want me, like, back in July. So and I was going, the trip in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to shortcut mine trip. I went to Israel with my daughter. And I came back-- couldn't come back by the Shalom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because Shalom was leaving two weeks later. And I had to be in Germany in July. So I went with the Greek boat. I came back with the Greek, with Olympia. And then I left mine daughter with my husband. And I flew to Germany. And this was a terrifying experience for me because I had to go to-- I don't remember the city, where this thing, where the court was. And I had to sleep over in a-- in a German family. I-- so it-- for me, it was a terrible thing. Anyway, when I came to that, I didn't know. I have somewhere, written down. You must have some information I-- I have it there. When I came on the Monday, to the court, you know, they picked me up and they took me. And they asked me if I recognized him. Naturally, I recognized him immediately. And it told me that he had a restaurant. He became an owner for a restaurant in-- in Hanover. And the-- anyway, I told them, everything, what I knew. And when he-- and he was put away. I don't know for how many, 20 years, whatever. I don't know what it was. I want to get out. Because I left my child in Europe. And I came in-- in America with my husband. So I was there for two days. I had to testify, two days. And I took a plane back straight home. And I didn't want to think about it. I cried and-- oh, yes. I was so upset. I-- I forgot to tell you that that I was, for two months, very ill. The doctor had to-- came to give me shots. Because all mine health, I had to talk about it in front of a court. And what that-- that murderer, who killed so many innocent people, women, baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And took me a few months to get it [INAUDIBLE]. But then-- then when was it, last time? I have written down. You have this. When was that again? '75? When did I? You had to go again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, you asked me those things. And I have written down, But I don't remember. Anyway, and-- here I am, 17 years. So let me say when it was. In the early '70s, I think it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got a letter again, from the Consulate. And they said they want to talk to me. And I said, what's about it? I wrote them. I called them. I said, why? They said, it's coming now, it seems that Helmut Schnabel, that the judge who put him away for life died. And they find some kind of excuse, some kind of-- in the law. And they let him free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says finally, they decided to put him away. But you are the only witness. Because most of them are dead. And there's only going to be like-- there was a young man from Israel, a young boy, maybe a witness's son or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to the consulate. And we-- and they came, the-- probably would be the-- the lawyer and the-- the one who puts it, what's called the other side? Prosecution?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The prosecutor and the lawyer and a judge. There came four people from the German court, special, to talk to me. And I came there. And we sat down and they talk to me. They ask me some questions, and I told them. And I figured that would be the end. And then I get a letter, a couple months later. They-- they put him, again, back in prison. And they need me, as to testify again. And I hated to go back. I'm very frank with you. I wanted to put it behind me. But since the way they told me that they don't have any witnesses. So I went back to Germany. I don't remember what day it was, anyway. I came then, in-- this time it was in Hanover. And you know, I got first-class round-trip ticket. I got a room in a hotel next to the-- to the court there. And they picked me up. You know, a car came, picked me up and brought me to the court. And he was in the back of the-- this is interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted the young kids to know what was going on, what their grandfathers did. You know? So there was a couple of different schools of children sitting in the back, in the courtroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there. And they brought him in, Schnabel, with two of the guards. And they asked me if I recognized him. Yeah. And then I-- I told them-- I didn't tell them everything. I mean, it would take, like here, for hours. I told them what I knew. And they thanked me. And they took me back to my hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that same evening, the representative committee, from the Jewish committee from Hanover, from the Jewish people, called me, if I would like to meet them. Because I told them I'm leaving. You know? So they picked me up. And I saw them with the representative from the Jewish-- what do they call it-- That Jewish representatives from-- from Germany, OK? And I think we had dinner, coffee. And we were talking. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were grateful and thanked me very much for mine coming and doing this. Because it was a great effort for me. It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not well, you know? And I had all this trouble here. And-- and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said goodbye. And I said yes. Before I left the court, I said to the judge then, and the prosecutor, I said, don't you ever, people, call me again if you're going to let him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will never come again. Never. That's what I told them. So when this Jewish representative committee asked me, Molly, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They let him out again. I said, I don't give a damn. You can shoot him. You can poison him. You can do what you want. I had it. I don't want, anymore. So six months later-- wait. That's not the end. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months later I got the letter from the Jewish Community of Germany, telling me that because of me, thanks to me, Helmut Schnabel got three life sentences, one after another. So they said to me, I think that you don't have to worry about [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I told them they could kill him or shoot, or do-- because why? Because after the war, Germany, and didn't have any more deaths.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's because, you know, they have so many Nazis there, thousands of Nazis. If they will put them all to death it will be terrifying. So they put a new law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only life sentence. No death. You see? So that's why Schnabel got three times, life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, don't worry. You don't have to come again. That's what I wanted to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I have that piece of paper, the newspaper, you know? And-- and I don't know where I put it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask you about coming to the United States. When did you come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came the 20-- this was just before Christmas, the 22nd, I think, of December, 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to adjust to life in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I got, right away, a job. We [INAUDIBLE] right for American Jewish Congress, in New York. And worked for five years, until my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for the American Jewish Congress?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do they do?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I was secretary to Mr-- the famous Jewish writer and poet, Mr. Jacob Glatshteyn. We used to do articles, releases, and current events in-- from English to Yiddish, and from Yiddish to English. I was to do translations and-- and I typed in the Yiddish, on a Yiddish typewriter and a Hebrew typewriter. So I used to do the typing in Hebrew. And then-- and I-- I would have-- they would have never let me go. But I got pregnant and I made my babies. So I had to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came because my brother brought me here, you see? I never saw my brother. That's the first time I saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he waited for me, for the-- by the boat, to [INAUDIBLE]. It's the first time I'll ever saw mine own brother. Because he was 23 years older than me, you see. And he came with his wife, I remember. And he drove in his Cadillac, from New York to Cleveland, Ohio, all day and all evening. We came in the middle of the night there, and stayed with him for a few months.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came to New York, about my job, you know, to find out, and get a Jewish typewriter, things like that, you know? And then I came to New York. And my husband came a year later because he didn't have the papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had the papers because of my brother, and then I got papers for him. But I couldn't-- the only thing, what was surprising to me, you know, when I went-- you went to East Side New York, all those places, it was dirty. It was so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was surprising to me. Because to me, United States was something like a paradise, you know? When I came.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Germany was so clean. Clean of humans and clean of everything, you know? This is-- but I wouldn't trade the United States for no country on this planet.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Did you have other jobs while your child was growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other jobs? Oh, I used to teach art off and on. And I went to art school anyway. Yeah, I had an art teacher in-- where I lived in New Rochelle, [? Madame Jeanette, ?] [? Lady Jeanette. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: She died many years ago. And she had a-- a studio. And she had-- and she used to teach-- we had-- she had about, I don't know, maybe 20 people. That's all students, you know? So I used to take art lessons. And I used to make portraits and make a few bucks, you know? Something. But not too many.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was busy with my child. With my past, I wanted to devote all my life to my child. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also do some writing? I-- I-- I can see that you've done some painting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I did-- I wrote some-- when I worked for the American Jewish Congress, mine-- my superior, Mr. Jacob Glashteyn, the famous poet and writer, was nagging me I should write a book. He wouldn't leave me in peace. He will come every day, sit. And I said to him, Mr. Glashteyn, this is my blood. I can't talk about my past. It hurt. Maybe even I will be old, I said. It would be like, 40, 50 years away. I will maybe be able to talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so the only thing he could do, since we-- we printed a monthly magazine, a cultural magazine with all the famous writers all over the world, and we send them all over the world in Yiddish. They translated it in their language. So I used to write articles-- single articles, what happened with me. You know? And I wrote about-- I don't know, as-- as many as the way-- we started this. I remember this magazine. I'm-- I'm thinking 1952, I think. For three years, we had it going. And then I left. You know? Having my baby. So there's a lot of articles.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you'd like to add before I ask my last question? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I add? I'm-- the only thing, what I hope and pray that this will never happen again. Because discrimination against any religion, in any culture, is a terrible thing. Because we all have the same rights to live and all the same right to die. If you don't like people, you don't have to-- you don't have to associate with them. But don't kill them. And the-- the Jewish people were killed because the Nazis didn't like the Jews. They wanted to take everything, what the Jews have. I don't know why. I don't know the reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: But an Aryan nation, they want only an Aryan nation in this world. And we have Muslims. And we have all kind of people living in-- in-- in the world, you know what I mean? So what can I say? I'm not-- well-- well, let's see, I'm not happy about a lot of things, at the present, what goes on in the world. But I give up. I'm old and it doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I only pray that my children and my grandchildren, and my great grandchildren will have a peaceful, happy life, that's all. And no matter where you stand, United States is still the best country in the world. Because no matter where you go-- and I went to visit Cuba, with my sister, before Castro. I know Germany. I know England. England's a nice place. England is similar to United States. I was in Canada. I was in Paris. I was in France. I-- I traveled all over the world. But I wouldn't settle no place except here. If I were a millionaire, maybe England. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You can visit Paris, France, and go to Moulin Rouge or all those places. But with mine experience, that's still the freest country in the world. And God bless America, OK? Thank you very much for doing the interview with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my mother, Leah Bastocka, maiden name, [? Plotnik. ?] And my father, Nosen best that Bastocky, Who died from a massive stroke. He was 52 years old. He died before Hitler killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when these pictures were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if I was born then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many, many years ago, before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where they were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vilna. Vilna, Poland. That time it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Julia. Julia Epstein. She left for Cuba when I was eight years old. And she came back to the States when Castro came to power, with her husband. And she has a daughter. And she's still alive. She's senile. She's 88 years old. She lives in Florida. She has a daughter, Mary Epstein, who is a Professor of Music. Her son-in-law is a scientist who he, with two more people, invented the toaster. And she has two granddaughters, my-- my-- my niece, Mary has two daughters in California, who are married and have children. But she is-- she doesn't know who she is anymore. She's-- for the last five years, she suffers from Alzheimer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, [? Rebekkah, ?] Rivka. And she was-- she once won a beauty contest. She was very beautiful. And she was-- she was killed by the Nazis, with her two sons and her husband, in [INAUDIBLE] ghetto, in 1943. Which is a little ghetto in the outskirts of Vilna. The Vilna ghetto was big. There was a little ghetto there. But this was a separate one. Because they used to make, first, what they caught, what they grabbed from the Jewish people who they killed. And remade it for the women in Germany, to send. And this was called [INAUDIBLE], in Lithuanian. I don't know what it means. But she was killed with her two sons and her husband. And my mother, they were staying with her. So they took her-- hard for me to talk about it. But took my mother on-- on-- on a-- on the train, with the old people somewhere, and they poisoned her. And I wasn't-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture, the center picture, the young girl with the braid is my sister, Chasia, who was killed in 1943, with her little baby. As she was with my sister, Rivka, living with then. Next to her is my brother, Itzak. He was shot dead, right on the streets of Vilna. And Chasia's husband was killed like my brother, on the streets of Vilna, in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken? Or where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Vilna, Poland. And I must have been-- I don't know. He-- she was about 10, 8 years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was like, 15. So I was like, seven years old, probably. Seven or eight years. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was eight years old when [INAUDIBLE] pictures when I was a kid. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Sam, who brought me to the States. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the shorter one.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left or the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the one in the uniform is his son. And he's-- my brother's dead. And his son is dead. But my brother was 23 years older than me. And he lived in the States. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the names, again? Your brother's name and the son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Bastocky.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my brother's name. And my-- and his son is Irwin Bastocky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Cleveland, Ohio. That's where we lived. He came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and what do you call? When the soldiers? And this was the war. He came on leave. Well, what do you call this? In the back of the picture, it should say something, there. OK. What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken from 1946. I don't-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: But no, who are the-- who are the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this, that was-- this is the British representative from UNRRA, for-- in 19 nations, or something like. I have written down, there. But nobody said. And it's-- I worked there, you see, as a translator in languages. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the center.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me. This is me, still, British. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now-- nobody--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to show you Mr. [? Kidman. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who's this is in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Molly Ingster. The next one is Miss. [? Steele. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the right is a representative from France, from the United Nations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945 '45 or '46. It should be on the picture, in the back there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is the building, the-- the back of the UNRRA Team 806, in Bergen-Belsen. And I am there, standing behind this-- with the plaque. UNRRA Team 806, taken by one of the British people. I was there. I was the only one survivor who worked with Mr. [? Kidman ?] and Mr. [? Blumberg. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1945 or '46. You should have this. They have the dates there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was Mr. [? Kidman's ?] secretary, on UNRRA Team 806. And we-- we used to drive around in his jeep, in Bergen-Belsen, check with people, if they have food enough and if they have-- if their houses are-- if they have places where to live, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the two in the jeep together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's me and Mr. [? Kidman. ?] Can you tell me about this please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I told you, that's all, what I remember. This is a bunch of survivors in Hanover. We had-- somebody had a birthday. And they invited me to come to the birthday party. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is-- is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? I am second from left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all, what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is Kramer, the Nazi SS with the British court. And hanged him after the liberation in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me the words in Yiddish on the side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. That's how it looked after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this a newspaper that was printed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was called 'Bergen-Belsen Paper, 1945, [INAUDIBLE].' That's when the British took the pictures, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the bottom part of this same paper, which was took before. And it shows the mass grave of the thousands of Jews who were buried after the liberation, died. And it shows this is shoes and pockets books, something, laying. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this is the skeletons of the people, in the mass graves, where-- and you can put down that Anne grave-- Anne Frank was in one of these mass graves. And she died after liberation, from typhoid, like the rest of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my work certificate from UNRRA team, when I worked with-- from 1945. I don't remember, till 1948. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was '46.

speaker: interviewee
text: '46. OK. I don't remember this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two weeks after the liberation, when I was standing by the-- the fence in Bergen-Belsen, an American tanker came by with the tank. They saw me standing there by the fence. They stopped the tank. And he came over. And he said, can I help you in any way? And I said to him, I would like to get in touch with member of the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't remember the others, exactly. So I-- I gave him-- I said that I know that they are in Cleveland, Ohio, and Sam Bastocky. And he got in touch to an organization and got in touch with my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an article written in Germany, in a German newspaper, after my testimony against the Nazi criminal, Helmut Schnabel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is July, 1965?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, July, 1965.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a magazine which was printed from the American Jewish Congress, in connection with World Jewish Congress. And I work, for five years, for the American Jewish Congress and World Congress. And my most immediate was Mr. Jacob Glashteyn. And he-- he did it, how do you say, the-- he [INAUDIBLE] it in English. He and me, we got all articles from all over the world for famous writer. And in this magazine is articles about me, about my past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody want me to-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is your article in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And-- and number 10 is mine article. I wrote this article. This is special, how I escape from the death transport, which I escape with my child the first I went-- went to Auschwitz-birkenau. And we went to the gas chambers. And sorry, it says that [NON-ENGLISH], how I escape from the death transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that what she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this article that you wrote.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I wrote an article about a broken-- called 'The Broken Spoon Which Saved My Life.' OK? And this was printed in South Africa, in Johannesburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an article written by the local newspaper, about me in 1983, when we got it in Washington, to put down the grand stones for building the Holocaust Museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my daughter, Lillian Ingster, who graduate from John Hopkins University in pre-med school, and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was 21 years. And she's 40. Well, 20 years ago now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my right side is [? Nathanial Moore, ?] my oldest grandson. My daughter's, Lillian, [INAUDIBLE] son. Nathanial, he's-- he's named after my father, Nosen. The middle one is [? Matthew Moore. ?] And the smallest one is [? Alex Moore. ?] And the middle one, this one is-- is Matthew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the one sitting on the left of the other two.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is Matthew. And in the center is Alex. And these are all mine three grandsons. Mine daughter's three grandchildren. My daughter's children, and my-- Lillian Ingster's three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken how many years ago? About?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five, four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four, five years ago.
